envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Pesticide and Other Agricultural Chemical Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat product sales under purchase orders, distributor programs, private-label or toll-formulation agreements, with pricing by product volume and formulation rather than labor hours.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal production, distributors without manufacturing, farm applicators, research-only entities, shell registrants, and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers and formulators of pesticides and other agricultural chemicals supplying repeat external agricultural, distribution, professional-use, or branded customers.

a.bridge: BLS provides occupational context for chemical manufacturing rather than NAICS 325320 alone, and O*NET identifies production-management work in reports, schedules, quality systems, inventory, audits, and compliance. I map the document-heavy and analytical subset to AI exposure while excluding physical processing, laboratory execution, maintenance, materials handling, and accountable release decisions; the ancestor mix may overstate plant scale and technical staffing at LMM formulators.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task study was found.
The frozen compensation ratio has a 2024-wage versus 2022-receipts vintage mismatch and a methodological harmonization adjustment.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted role and task census from 15-25 independent pesticide formulators, including time spent on regulatory, quality, commercial, laboratory, packaging, and plant work, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Regulatory submissions, label and safety-document maintenance, quality records, forecasting, procurement support, customer service, and sales administration are exposed. Formulation, mixing, packaging, testing, maintenance, warehousing, and hazardous-material controls remain physical or judgment-intensive.

caveats: No directly observed five-year volume forecast for the exact service basket was found.
EPA's recurring reviews can remove uses or impose mitigations while also sustaining compliance barriers.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-price volume forecast by herbicide, insecticide, fungicide, biological, adjuvant, and other agricultural-chemical category, reconciled to USDA application acres and manufacturer shipments, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Crop protection and agricultural input needs are recurring, but planted acreage, farm economics, weather, pest pressure, resistance, regulation, biological alternatives, and integrated pest management can shift volumes. The base case assumes broadly stable constant-price demand with modest mix-driven growth, not nominal chemical-price inflation.

caveats: The assigned count of 33 is an estimate based on a margin bridge, not an observed EBITDA-band census.
A registrant, establishment, product label, and operating company are different units.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census linking SUSB-band manufacturers to EPA establishment and registration records, ownership, revenue model, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Most independent manufacturers sell repeatedly to external distributors, growers, professional users, or brands, so the recurring external-customer lens is natural. Captive plants, pure IP or registration vehicles, highly project-based development shops, and firms whose normalized EBITDA is outside the band reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325320 ($1,469,150,817) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325320 ($18,819,201,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $1,569,837 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325320); class <5: 67 firms, avg revenue/firm $940,776 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $169,434 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,250,720 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $405,355 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,447,520 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $981,098 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,039,758 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,230,060 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $151,322,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $27,253,207 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $594,845,958 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,131,757 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 33

caveats: This is operator requirement for the current manufactured basket, not the share of all pest management that uses chemicals.
Contract manufacturing can shift which operator supplies the product without eliminating operator requirement.
o.change_evidence: Customer substitution studies and shipment data measuring migration from conventional manufactured products to self-produced, nonchemical, or software-only pest-control methods would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Year-five demand for physical formulated chemicals still requires a registered, quality-controlled manufacturer or formulator. AI can assist development and administration but cannot replace chemical production, packaging, testing, traceability, and regulatory accountability; some quantity can migrate to on-farm practices, biological controls, or integrated solutions outside the current basket.

caveats: No representative contract sample was available.
Retention differs between proprietary products, generic formulations, and toll manufacturing.
q.change_evidence: Customer and toll-manufacturing contracts showing cost-plus clauses, price-review cadence, rebates, rebids, and gross-margin behavior after productivity projects would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Product and formulation pricing is generally not contractually tied to the producer's labor cost, permitting savings retention. Over five years, distributor bargaining, private-label rebids, generic competition, and customer price negotiation should share some benefit, while registration and quality barriers can slow pass-through.

caveats: Five-year implementation performance specific to LMM pesticide manufacturers is unobserved.
Readiness varies with ERP, laboratory, and registration-data quality.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after labor hours, error rates, deviations, and regulatory outcomes from pesticide manufacturers using AI in quality, regulatory affairs, demand planning, and customer operations would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: AI assistants can be implemented in document search, drafting, forecasting, customer support, and exception triage, but validated source data, cybersecurity, label control, quality systems, toxicology evidence, and human signoff constrain deployment. EPA's detailed data and review obligations make unsupervised automation inappropriate.

caveats: No pesticide-manufacturer owner-age or qualifying-transfer panel was found.
Product portfolio divestitures must not be counted as control transfers of firms.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, EPA company-number changes, and state establishment-license ownership changes reconciled to the 33 estimated firms would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Registrations, formulations, customer relationships, and compliant plants create transferable going-concern value, supporting ordinary private-company succession and strategic acquisitions. However, no observed five-year transfer denominator for eligible LMM firms was found, and some transactions are product or registration transfers rather than control sales.

principal_driver: Repeat regulated product demand with pricing that is not directly indexed to producer labor cost.
principal_weakness: Low compensation intensity and stringent human-accountability requirements limit the practical size of AI-driven labor savings.
